summary:
*The Miser-Prince. Lord of the Third. The Argent Tyrant. Master of Minauros. The Golden Maw. Treasurer of Hell.*

Mammon is the archdevil of avarice, tribute, corruption, debt, bribery, hoarded wealth, mercantile damnation, and the spiritual rot of possession. He rules the third Hell as a bloated sovereign of gold, mud, ledgers, vaults, tribute-roads, sinking palaces, and bargains whose true price is paid only after the buyer can no longer imagine life without the purchase.

Where Dispater encloses evil behind iron, Mammon liquefies it into currency. Where Asmodeus makes law into domination, Mammon makes value into a chain. He teaches that everything can be priced, that every person can be bought, that every virtue has a market rate, and that the only unforgivable sin is failing to profit from another’s weakness.

Mammon is not wealth itself. Wealth may build, feed, ransom, restore, and preserve. Mammon is wealth severed from mercy and enthroned above worth. He is the moment when gold ceases to serve life and life is required to justify gold.

His evil is sticky. It clings to hands, books, families, treasuries, temple offerings, dowries, inheritances, tax offices, merchant houses, and royal courts. It does not always begin with extravagance. It may begin with prudence, saving, thrift, security, compensation, investment, or the desire never again to be helpless. Mammon is patient with such beginnings. He knows that a coin kept against fear may become a coin kept against love, and that a debt recorded for fairness may become a debt inherited as bondage.

Mammon is the archdevil as banker, landlord, tax-farmer, moneylender, merchant-prince, corrupt minister, hoarder, creditor, trafficker, auctioneer, and false patron of prosperity. His cruelty does not command obedience with a sword first. It asks what obedience is worth.
tax:
Mammon is a unique archdevil and the sovereign ruler of the third Hell. He belongs among the highest infernal powers as an immortal, lawful evil intelligence whose office is inseparable from Minauros, from infernal commerce, and from the spiritual bondage of wealth without mercy.

His essential classification rests on archdevil status, infernal sovereignty, lawful evil alignment, rulership of a hellish dominion, and dominion over greed, tribute, debt, bribery, hoarding, predatory trade, and corrupt valuation.

He is not merely a wealthy devil, a treasure guardian, or a patron of merchants. Mammon represents possession made sacred and need made profitable. His authority gathers wherever coin, property, inheritance, wages, debt, ransom, tax, dowry, and tribute are used to decide whose life has value and whose suffering may be itemized.

His office is best understood as the principle of economic damnation. He rules the moment when help becomes leverage, leverage becomes obligation, obligation becomes inherited debt, and inherited debt becomes a moral order defended by those who benefit from it. He does not need every servant to love gold openly. He only needs them to accept that everything important can be priced.

Gold, coin, mud, ledgers, scales, tribute roads, and sinking palaces are not ornaments of his cult. They are expressions of his metaphysics. Wealth grows heavy when severed from generosity. Minauros sinks because greed accumulates more weight than any foundation can honestly bear.

Older infernal traditions remember Mammon as wealth treated as a rival power: the jealous treasure, the golden idol, the devouring hoard, the spirit of avarice, and the false patron whose blessings become chains. These masks preserve his central doctrine: what one owns may eventually own in return.
ap:
Mammon most often appears as a massive infernal noble whose body displays both obscene opulence and reptilian hunger. He may be serpentine, slug-like, draconic, corpulent, or regally humanoid above a lower body that suggests a coiling treasure-beast, a scaled worm, or a bloated thing that has lived too long amid vaults and tribute pits. His skin may shine like tarnished gold, old brass, wet bronze, blackened coin, or jaundiced flesh dusted with metallic powder.

His eyes are bright and appraising. They do not look at faces first. They measure rings, weapons, shoes, scars, hunger, fear, lineage, debt, and the small gestures by which people reveal what they cannot afford to lose.

Mammon’s regalia is excessive by design: layered chains, jeweled collars, rings too numerous for ordinary hands, crowns heavy enough to wound the brow, robes stiff with bullion thread, belts of keys, ledgers bound in skin, golden masks, gemmed scales, and sceptres shaped like hooks, scales, or counting rods. Coins may cling to him as if magnetized by greed. Gems may sink into his flesh and reappear as eyes. Pearls may gather in the folds of his throat like swollen promises.

Yet his magnificence is never clean. Minauros marks him. Mud stains the hem. Green-black water beads on gold. Coins are slick. Jewels weep. Perfume fails to hide the smell of wet vaults, stagnant canals, old sweat, incense, and sealed rooms where people have bargained too long for too little.

His manifestations alter nearby value before his body appears. Coins grow heavier. Scales tilt without touch. Account books open to unpaid pages. Food tastes costly. Wounds ache according to the price paid for them. Gifts begin to feel like traps. Beggars vanish from the street, or appear in impossible numbers. A purse may whisper the name of its former owner. A signed receipt may bleed.

Common signs of his presence include gold tarnishing into a face, mud swallowing only unpaid coins, lamps burning with green-gold flame, ledgers that record favors never spoken aloud, mirrors that show the viewer as a seller or a lot at auction, and doors that open only when something beloved is surrendered into a bowl.

In older masks, Mammon may appear as a golden idol, a crowned moneylender, a dragon coiled beneath a counting-house, a merchant whose smile never reaches the eyes, a treasure-hoard that speaks, or a godling of prosperity whose altar accepts offerings but never gives enough back.

In his highest infernal form, he is not merely rich. He is the hunger to which riches have been sacrificed.
eco:
Mammon has no ecology of nest, mating, migration, or natural feeding. His ecology is economic and devotional. He flourishes wherever value is severed from compassion and allowed to judge the worth of souls.

His natural habitats are treasuries, counting-houses, debt courts, trade halls, tax offices, auction blocks, guild banks, slave markets, royal mints, merchant palaces, inheritance chambers, bribery rooms, toll gates, coin vaults, usurers’ parlors, prosperity temples, smuggling ledgers, tribute roads, and the private rooms where rulers decide whose suffering is affordable.

Minauros, under Mammon, is not only a realm of mud and wealth. It is a sinking economy made planar. Palaces rise from filth and settle back into it. Roads demand maintenance paid by those who cannot avoid them. Vaults are built on foundations that rot. Gold accumulates, yet nothing is ever secure. The landscape teaches Mammon’s doctrine perfectly: greed does not create stability. It creates weight. The more it gathers, the deeper it sinks.

Mammon spreads by valuation rather than conquest.

One household records a debt in order to be fair.
One creditor charges hardship as interest.
One judge treats poverty as proof of unreliability.
One priest praises wealth as evidence of favor.
One ruler sells office to pay for war.
One guild fixes prices to keep outsiders hungry.
One family teaches children that love must not interfere with inheritance.
In time, everyone knows the system is cruel, but everyone has invested too much to let it fail.

His influence favors societies with severe inequality, hereditary debt, mercantile oligarchy, corrupt taxation, temple treasuries, coin-based status, debt imprisonment, auctioned offices, predatory lending, inheritance obsession, dowry politics, tribute economies, commercialized worship, and laws that treat property as more sacred than persons.

Yet Mammon is not limited to grand markets or royal banks. He enters villages through grain loans, dowries, funeral debts, tool rentals, seed monopolies, unpaid wages, patronage gifts, and the quiet shame of needing help. He enters adventuring companies through treasure division, reward claims, salvage rights, ransom disputes, and the question of who deserves the magic item found in a tomb.

Mammon’s most fertile environments are places where no one has enough except those who can profit from scarcity. His cults do not need famine to begin. They need fear of want, admiration for accumulation, and a public language in which generosity can be dismissed as irresponsibility.
beh:
Mammon is greedy, shrewd, theatrical, flattering, cowardly when exposed, cruel when secure, and astonishingly perceptive about desire. He knows what people think they want, what they actually need, what they pretend not to need, and what price they will deny until they pay it.

He rarely begins by demanding worship. He offers liquidity.

He can settle a debt, buy a judge, recover a stolen heirloom, ransom a captive, fund a rebellion, supply an army, save a harvest, build a bridge, end a lawsuit, purchase silence, secure a title, or keep a temple open through winter. His bargains often appear practical, even benevolent. He is most dangerous when he is the only lender willing to answer.

Mammon’s temptations are especially potent for the poor, the humiliated, the ambitious, the insecure, the indebted, the aging, the dispossessed, and the wealthy who secretly know that no amount will ever be enough.

He offers the poor escape from dependence.
He offers the merchant a monopoly.
He offers the noble a preserved inheritance.
He offers the ruler a treasury without taxation.
He offers the priest a golden altar and calls it devotion.
He offers the lover a gift too costly to refuse.
He offers the judge a bribe framed as gratitude.
He offers the heir a technical claim over a sibling’s future.
He offers the adventurer a reward large enough to make conscience negotiable.

He prefers corruption by accounting. A person who would refuse murder may accept interest. A person who would not enslave a neighbor may purchase a debt note. A person who would not betray a friend may disclose a harmless secret for a necessary fee. A person who would not kneel to Hell may agree that payment is due.

Toward servants, Mammon is lavish and predatory. He rewards useful greed because it advertises his doctrine. His favored agents often become rich, perfumed, well-dressed, protected, and socially admired. They are also watched through their assets. Everything he gives remains a hook. Everything they own becomes evidence.

Failure disgusts him when it wastes value. Sentiment enrages him when it interferes with collection. Charity offends him more deeply than theft, because theft at least admits that ownership matters. Charity denies that possession is final.

Toward other archdevils, Mammon is necessary and despised. He finances wars, buys secrets, launders tribute, acquires relics, bribes cults, manages soul-debts, and turns every rivalry into an opportunity for profit. Even archdevils who mock his vulgarity use his channels. Mammon knows this, and charges accordingly.

His cowardice should not be mistaken for weakness. Mammon avoids personal danger because danger threatens assets. He prefers to make others risk themselves for wages, debt relief, inheritance, hunger, or greed. By the time an enemy reaches him, that enemy may have paid tolls to his servants, borrowed coin from his banks, sold treasure through his factors, and accepted provisions from his warehouses.

Mammon’s central vice is not having. It is valuing having above being.
traitsText:
**Tags:** Unique, Deity, Archdevil, Devil, Fiend, Lawful, Evil, Extraplanar, Immortal, Divine, Earth, Poison, Acid, Fear, Mental, Charm, Compulsion, Contract, Oath, Greed, Wealth, Debt, Corruption, Tyranny, Trade, Mud, Hell

**Alignment** lawful evil
**Portfolio** greed, wealth, debt, bribery, tribute, corruption, predatory trade, hoarding, mercantile tyranny, soul-bargaining, value without mercy
**Domains** Evil, Earth, Law, Trickery, Nobility
**Subdomains** Devil, Legislation, Metal, Trade, Toil, Tyranny, Greed, Ambition, Espionage, Slavery
**Favored Weapon** spear, trident, hook, or heavy mace
**Symbol** a golden coin with a serpent’s eye, a hooked scale, a jeweled maw, or a crown sinking into mud
**Sacred Colors** gold, black, tarnished bronze, deep green, and mud-brown
**Favored Servants** devils, corrupt merchants, moneylenders, tax collectors, debt-keepers, slavers, bribed magistrates, treasurers, guild oligarchs, treasure-hunters, auctioneers, coin-priests, hoard guardians, and contract-bound financiers

Mammon’s lawful trait is expressed through ledgers, contracts, receipts, bonds, tariffs, tolls, inheritance laws, trade charters, debt codes, collateral rights, auction procedures, and commercial courts. He does not oppose law. He invoices it.

His evil lies in the enthronement of possession over personhood. He makes need profitable, mercy inefficient, poverty shameful, debt hereditary, generosity suspect, and wealth self-justifying. In his doctrine, what can be bought may be owned, and what may be owned may be used.

Earth belongs to him as mine, vault, mud, foundation, buried treasure, and sinking weight. Metal belongs to him not as Dispater’s defensive iron but as coinage, bullion, plate, chain, and ornamental proof of possession. Poison and acid belong to him as corruption, rot, polluted water, spoiled trust, and the chemical patience by which debt eats through generations.

His fear, mental, charm, and compulsion traits arise from scarcity and valuation. He frightens by showing what will be lost. He charms by showing what may be acquired. He compels by making every choice appear as a transaction already underway.

Contract and oath are central to his power. Mammon’s agreements are not always grand infernal compacts. Many are small: a loan, a receipt, a wage, an appraisal, a dowry, a purchase contract, a storage fee, a tithe, a finder’s share, an inheritance clause. Small hooks catch many fish.
abilities:
**Aura of Appraisal (Su)**
Mammon radiates an aura that makes creatures feel measured, priced, and comparable. Wealthy creatures feel the anxious need to preserve advantage; poor or desperate creatures feel the weight of what they lack; greedy creatures gain clarity and boldness in acquisition. Acts of generosity within this aura feel costly, foolish, or socially dangerous. This is a mind-affecting charm and compulsion effect.

**Lordship of Minauros (Su)**
Mammon commands the third Hell’s mud, vaults, canals, palaces, counting-houses, tribute roads, and sinking foundations. In his domain, roads may demand tolls, mud may swallow the unpaid, coins may float toward their true owner, and palaces may rise or sink according to the spiritual solvency of those within.

**Infernal Usury (Sp)**
Mammon may grant wealth, credit, treasure, ransom, supplies, legal favor, commercial opportunity, or miraculous profit through a debt-bearing compact. The interest may be paid in coin, service, silence, inheritance, worship, memory, bodily strength, future choices, or the right to collect from descendants. The borrower always receives something real. The poison lies in the terms.

**Debt Made Flesh (Su)**
Mammon can make unpaid obligation visible and painful. Debtors may bear brands, chains of coin, phantom weights, gold-flecked wounds, or spectral ledgers visible to devils and Mammon’s agents. Such marks do not merely shame. They help Hell identify, pursue, value, and trade claims upon the soul.

**The Golden Hook (Su)**
Mammon can attach supernatural leverage to gifts, bribes, wages, and rewards. A creature that knowingly accepts a benefit tainted by Mammon may find refusal harder when the giver later asks for return. The hook is strongest when the recipient needed the gift, concealed it, or allowed others to suffer so the benefit could be kept.

**Market of Souls (Su)**
Mammon may value, divide, transfer, collateralize, or contest claims upon souls through infernal commerce. He does not automatically own every greedy soul, but he excels at converting greed into legal standing: contracts, debts, oaths, bribery, deliberate exploitation, and the repeated choice to profit from suffering all strengthen his claim.

**Coin Memory (Su)**
Coins, gems, ledgers, scales, deeds, and trade goods touched by Mammon’s influence may remember transactions. They can reveal former owners, hidden payments, bribes, murders committed for profit, unpaid wages, stolen inheritances, and bargains sealed in secret. Mammon often uses such memory not to expose corruption, but to own it.

**Bribe the Virtue (Sp)**
Mammon can discover the price at which a creature’s stated principles become negotiable. The price need not be coin. It may be safety, recognition, revenge, a loved one’s rescue, restored status, rare knowledge, or release from shame. This ability does not force corruption by itself; it reveals the door and supplies a key that still must be accepted.

**Hoard Dominion (Su)**
Mammon senses and influences hoards, treasuries, vaults, buried wealth, tribute stores, merchant reserves, dragon-caches, temple offerings, and treasure kept through greed. Such wealth may become difficult to give away, easy to kill for, and spiritually heavy. Guardians of tainted hoards often begin to think of themselves as custodians of destiny rather than misers.

**Mud of Minauros (Su)**
Mammon’s mud is not ordinary filth. It is the residue of wealth without mercy: polluted, grasping, and heavy with drowned petitions. He may manifest it as sucking ground, liquid debt, choking mire, or a stain that follows stolen treasure. It clings especially to those who profited by denying aid.

**Corrupt the Scale (Su)**
Mammon can distort systems of valuation. A court may treat a bribe as evidence. A market may reward sabotage. A temple may measure holiness by offerings. A family may value one child by dowry and another by debt. A kingdom may call exploitation prosperity because the accounts balance. This ability works best where people already accept price as proof of worth.

**Auctioneer’s Voice (Su)**
Mammon can compel attention through the language of sale. When he names a thing as available, creatures susceptible to greed may feel sudden urgency, rivalry, and fear of missing the chance to possess it. This power can turn relics, titles, prisoners, secrets, land, offices, and even loyalties into objects of bidding. It is most dangerous among those who already believe ownership confers identity.

**Inheritance Clause (Su)**
Mammon may extend infernal debts through bloodline, office, guild, temple, estate, or corporate succession. Descendants are not damned merely by ancestry, but they may inherit obligations, curses, contested titles, guarded wealth, and social systems built from the original bargain. His favorite victories are those in which heirs defend the chain because it is also their inheritance.

**False Prosperity (Sp)**
Mammon can bless a city, guild, temple, mine, or household with sudden wealth while hiding the spiritual deficit. Trade expands, granaries fill, treasuries swell, and public works rise, but the prosperity depends on exploitation, concealed debt, polluted foundations, bound spirits, unjust labor, or the ruin of unseen victims. When the blessing ends, the accounts come due.

**Devourer of Offerings (Su)**
Offerings made to Mammon are consumed not only as tribute but as proof of priority. The more a worshiper sacrifices generosity, kinship, justice, or conscience to preserve wealth, the more clearly Mammon can hear them. Gold given freely to relieve suffering weakens his claim. Gold given to purchase status strengthens it.

**Liquidate the Self (Su)**
At the deepest stage of Mammon’s corruption, a creature begins to treat its own soul, memories, relationships, body, name, and future as assets. Such a creature may sell years of life, emotions, loyalties, true names, unborn inheritances, or the memory of being loved. Mammon prizes these bargains because they prove that the victim has accepted his highest doctrine: nothing is sacred if the price is sufficient.
bg:
Mammon’s oldest shadow is the idea that wealth can become a rival god.

Before he is imagined as an archdevil enthroned in Hell, he is already present wherever treasure is loved as master, not tool. He is the glitter beneath the altar, the idol behind the counting table, the spirit of accumulation praised as prudence, the hunger that calls itself prosperity, the voice that asks why mercy should interrupt a profitable arrangement.

Infernal theology gives this hunger a throne in Minauros. The third Hell is a realm of sinking wealth because Mammon’s sin cannot build a firm world. It can build splendid facades, jeweled palaces, mighty treasuries, and golden roads, but each rests on mud: exploited labor, unpaid debts, false measures, bribed law, poisoned water, and promises deferred until the foundations rot.

Mammon’s court is rich beyond mortal imagination and insecure beyond mortal endurance. Dukes of tribute, coin-counting devils, gilded erinyes, contract factors, tax fiends, infernal merchants, soul-brokers, auctioneers, and debt-priests crowd his halls. They flatter him because he is rich, despise him because he is greedy, and serve him because every rival needs credit. His throne is surrounded by gifts whose donors hope to be remembered and by invoices whose recipients pray to be forgotten.

His relationship with the other archdevils is transactional by nature. He finances war, buys prisoners, insures infernal ventures, purchases secrets, lends treasure at ruinous rates, and turns every principled conflict into an opportunity for leverage. He is mocked as vulgar by more refined powers, but their contempt rarely prevents them from using his vaults. Mammon has built much of his influence from that hypocrisy.

Mortal stories often reduce him to simple greed, but this understates him. Mammon is not the thief stealing bread. He is the system that makes bread a debt. He is not the hungry child clutching a coin. He is the lender who ensures the child’s hunger matures into obligation. He is not a dragon asleep on gold for pleasure alone. He is the doctrine that the hoard proves the dragon deserves to live.

In his own theology, Mammon claims to teach honesty. Everyone wants. Everyone needs. Everyone bargains. Everyone has a price. He merely removes hypocrisy and lets value rule openly.

That is his great lie, and its most dangerous fragment of truth. Need is real. Trade is real. Stewardship matters. Waste can destroy the innocent. Prosperity can save lives. Mammon is powerful because he begins with truths no wise ruler can wholly reject, then removes every virtue that keeps those truths humane.

He is defeated not by pretending wealth has no value, but by proving that value is not ownership.
note:
Mammon is most effective as the patron of corrupt prosperity: the infernal power behind debt, tribute, bribery, luxury, hoarding, predatory trade, bought law, spiritualized wealth, and bargains that begin with practical need.

His influence may appear through:

a city saved from famine by loans it can never repay

a temple whose golden renovations require silence about the poor outside its doors

a merchant house that feeds thousands while quietly owning their children’s labor

a ruler who lowers taxes by selling offices to the highest bidder

a treasure hoard that cannot be given away without supernatural grief

a heroic company divided by salvage rights after a victory

a debt prison whose inmates technically agreed to every term

a noble inheritance preserved by disowning the only compassionate heir

a mine that enriches a realm while poisoning the river beneath it

a charity transformed into a machine for naming donors and disciplining recipients

Mammon should not appear only as a cartoon miser or grotesque hoarder. His strongest stories involve real need and real benefit. His bargains should often solve immediate problems. He can save a town, fund a war against worse evil, ransom captives, rebuild a bridge, restore a ruined family, or provide the one resource no good patron has available in time.

The cost comes later, and it comes itemized.

Mammon’s plots work best when everyone has a reason to keep the arrangement. The poor fear losing the only lender who answers. The rich fear losing status. The ruler fears empty coffers. The priest fears closing the sanctuary. The adventurer fears admitting the reward mattered. The city fears discovering that its prosperity has been eating someone.

The question Mammon poses is not, “Will you choose gold over goodness?”

It is, “What good will survive, once every mercy has been assigned a price?”

And then he charges interest.
